title: Word Problems
blurb: Translate English into algebra and master rate, work, mixture, age, and integer setups fast.
theory:
What Word Problems Really Test

Every GRE word problem is a translation exercise. The hard part is rarely the algebra — it is turning English sentences into equations. Your job is to assign variables, write relationships, then solve. Master a handful of templates (rate, work, mixture, age, integers) and most problems become routine.

Translating English to Algebra

Build a habit of converting key phrases word-by-word:

EnglishAlgebra
is, was, will be, equals=
more than, sum, increased by+
less than, fewer, decreased by−
of, times, product, twice×
per, ratio, quotient÷
a number, whatx

Watch order with less than: "7 less than x" is x − 7, not 7 − x. Define your variable explicitly ("let x = the number") so you never lose track of what you solved for.

Rate–Time–Distance: d = rt

The master formula is distance = rate × time. Rearrange as needed: r = d/t and t = d/r. Always keep units consistent (convert minutes to hours, etc.).

  • Average speed is total distance ÷ total time — never the simple average of two speeds. If you go 120 miles at 40 mph then 120 miles at 60 mph, time = 3 + 2 = 5 hours, so average speed = 240/5 = 48 mph (not 50).
  • Two travelers toward each other: their speeds add. Time to meet = total distance ÷ (r1 + r2).
  • Two travelers same direction (one chasing): the gap closes at the difference of speeds, r1 − r2.

Work-Rate Problems

If one worker finishes a job in a hours, their rate is 1/a job per hour. Combined rates add:

1/a + 1/b = 1/T, where T is the combined time.

So T = 1 ÷ (1/a + 1/b). Example: A finishes in 4 hours (rate 1/4), B in 6 hours (rate 1/6). Combined rate = 1/4 + 1/6 = 5/12, so T = 12/5 = 2.4 hours. A draining pipe works against filling, so you subtract: net rate = fill rate − drain rate.

Mixture & Concentration

Track the amount of pure substance, not just the volume. For a solution: pure amount = concentration × volume. Set up: (pure from A) + (pure from B) = (pure in final mixture). Adding pure substance counts as 100%; adding water counts as 0%.

Ages

Define present ages with variables. For past or future, add or subtract the same number of years from every person. "In 5 years" means each age becomes (age + 5); "4 years ago" means (age − 4). Translate "twice as old" carefully — it multiplies the other person's age.

Consecutive Integers

Represent consecutive integers as n, n+1, n+2, … For consecutive even or odd integers, use n, n+2, n+4, … (gap of 2). The sum of k consecutive integers equals k times the middle (mean) value, which makes many problems instant.

One vs Two Variables

Use one variable when a single unknown drives the rest. Use two variables when two quantities are independent — then you need two equations (substitution or elimination). Don't over-introduce variables; fewer is faster.

Strategy: Picking Numbers and Back-Solving

When answers contain variables or percents, pick smart numbers (100 for percents, a common multiple for fractions) and compute concretely. When answers are specific numbers, back-solve by plugging choices into the problem — start with the middle choice (C) since answers are usually ordered, so one test tells you which direction to go.
whatEtsTests:
• Translating multi-step English sentences into one or two equations accurately, especially the order in 'less than' and 'the quotient of'.
  • Rate problems using d = rt, with average speed = total distance ÷ total time (the classic 'is it just the average?' trap).
  • Combined work rate via 1/a + 1/b = 1/T, including pipes that fill and drain simultaneously.
  • Mixture problems tracking the amount of pure substance across two solutions or added water/concentrate.
  • Age relationships shifted into past or future, applied equally to all parties.
  • Recognizing when picking numbers or back-solving from answer choices is faster than algebra.
tips: Average speed = total distance ÷ total time. Never average the two speeds unless the times are equal.
For percent-change answers, pick 100 as the starting value; a 20% rise then 20% fall lands at 96, a net 4% loss.
Back-solve numeric answer choices starting from the middle (C). The result tells you to go up or down, often solving in one or two tests.
For combined work, add rates not times: 1/a + 1/b = 1/T. A drain subtracts: net rate = fill − drain.
In mixture problems, write one equation in terms of pure substance: c1·V1 + c2·V2 = cfinal·Vfinal.
For consecutive-integer sums, the sum equals (count) × (middle value), so the average is the middle term — read off the answer directly.
traps: Averaging two speeds directly instead of using total distance over total time.
Reversing 'less than': writing 7 − x when '7 less than x' means x − 7.
Adding times instead of rates in work problems (two 4-hour workers do NOT finish in 8 hours — they finish in 2).
Forgetting to apply the time shift to every person in age problems, or shifting only one age.
In mixtures, tracking total volume instead of the amount of pure substance, or treating added water as nonzero concentration.

examples.q: The sum of three consecutive integers is 51. What is the largest of the three integers?
examples.steps:
1. Let the integers be n, n+1, n+2.

2. Sum: n + (n+1) + (n+2) = 3n + 3 = 51.

3. Solve: 3n = 48, so n = 16.

4. The integers are 16, 17, 18; the largest is 18.
examples.shortcut: The sum of three consecutive integers is 3 times the middle one: 51 ÷ 3 = 17 is the middle, so the largest is 17 + 1 = 18.

examples.q: A car travels 120 miles at 40 miles per hour, then travels another 120 miles at 60 miles per hour. What is the car's average speed for the entire trip, in miles per hour?
examples.steps:
1. Time for first leg: 120 ÷ 40 = 3 hours.

2. Time for second leg: 120 ÷ 60 = 2 hours.

3. Total distance = 240 miles; total time = 3 + 2 = 5 hours.

4. Average speed = 240 ÷ 5 = 48 mph.
examples.shortcut: Don't average 40 and 60 to get 50 — that's the trap. Because more time is spent at the slower speed, the average must be below 50. Only 48 fits.

examples.q: Pump A can fill a tank in 4 hours and pump B can fill the same tank in 6 hours. Working together, how many hours do they take to fill the tank?
examples.steps:
1. Rate of A = 1/4 tank/hour; rate of B = 1/6 tank/hour.

2. Combined rate = 1/4 + 1/6. Common denominator 12: 3/12 + 2/12 = 5/12 tank/hour.

3. Time = 1 ÷ (5/12) = 12/5 hours.

4. T = 2.4 hours.
examples.shortcut: Combined time is always less than the faster individual time (4 hours), so it must be under 4 — eliminates any larger choice immediately.

examples.q: A chemist has 10 liters of a 20% acid solution. How many liters of pure acid must be added to make a solution that is 50% acid?
examples.steps:
1. Pure acid now = 0.20 × 10 = 2 liters.

2. Let x = liters of pure acid added. New pure acid = 2 + x; new total volume = 10 + x.

3. Equation: (2 + x)/(10 + x) = 0.50.

4. Cross-multiply: 2 + x = 5 + 0.5x, so 0.5x = 3, giving x = 6 liters.
examples.shortcut: Pure acid is 100% concentration. Each liter added raises both the acid and the total, so test x = 6: (2+6)/(10+6) = 8/16 = 50%. Confirmed.

questions.q: Five more than twice a number is 23. What is the number?
A car travels at a constant speed of 60 miles per hour for 2.5 hours. How many miles does it travel?
The sum of two consecutive even integers is 46. What is the smaller integer?
questions.explain: Let the integers be n and n+2. Then n + (n+2) = 46, so 2n + 2 = 46 and 2n = 44, giving n = 22 (the integers are 22 and 24).

questions.q: Anna is twice as old as Beth. The sum of their ages is 36 years. How old is Anna, in years?
questions.explain: Let Beth = b, so Anna = 2b. Then b + 2b = 36, giving 3b = 36 and b = 12. Anna = 2 × 12 = 24.

questions.q: A train leaves City X heading toward City Y at 40 miles per hour. At the same moment, a train leaves City Y heading toward City X at 60 miles per hour. The cities are 300 miles apart. How many hours after they depart do the two trains meet?
questions.explain: Moving toward each other, the trains close the gap at 40 + 60 = 100 miles per hour. Time = distance ÷ combined speed = 300 ÷ 100 = 3 hours.

questions.q: A store raises the price of an item by 20%, then later reduces the new price by 20%. The final price is what percent of the original price?
questions.explain: Pick 100 for the original price. After +20%: 120. After −20% of 120: 120 × 0.80 = 96. The final price is 96% of the original (a net 4% decrease). The trap is thinking the changes cancel to 100%.

questions.q: Worker A can paint a fence in 5 hours and worker B can paint the same fence in 4 hours. Working together at their constant rates, how long will they take to paint the fence?
questions.explain: Rates add: 1/5 + 1/4 = 4/20 + 5/20 = 9/20 fence per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (9/20) = 20/9 hours (about 2.22 hours). It must be less than the faster worker's 4 hours.

questions.qa: Time to travel 100 miles at 50 miles per hour
questions.qb: Time to travel 150 miles at 100 miles per hour
questions.explain: Quantity A: 100 ÷ 50 = 2 hours. Quantity B: 150 ÷ 100 = 1.5 hours. Since 2 > 1.5, Quantity A is greater (choice A).

questions.q: How many liters of pure water must be added to 5 liters of a 40% salt solution to dilute it to a 25% salt solution?
questions.explain: Salt amount = 0.40 × 5 = 2 liters and stays fixed (water has no salt). Need 2/(5 + x) = 0.25, so 5 + x = 8 and x = 3 liters.

questions.q: A pipe can fill a tank in 6 hours. A drain at the bottom can empty the full tank in 9 hours. If both the pipe and the drain are open at the same time, how many hours does it take to fill the empty tank?
questions.explain: Net filling rate = fill − drain = 1/6 − 1/9. Common denominator 18: 3/18 − 2/18 = 1/18 tank per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (1/18) = 18 hours.

questions.q: A chemist mixes a 30% acid solution with a 60% acid solution to obtain 50 liters of a 48% acid solution. How many liters of the 30% solution are used?
questions.explain: Let x = liters of 30% solution, so 50 − x liters of 60% solution. Acid balance: 0.30x + 0.60(50 − x) = 0.48 × 50 = 24. Then 0.30x + 30 − 0.60x = 24, so −0.30x = −6 and x = 20 liters.

questions.q: Alex is 5 years older than Ben. Four years ago, Alex was twice as old as Ben was. How old is Alex now, in years?
questions.explain: Let Ben = b now, so Alex = b + 5. Four years ago: (b + 5) − 4 = 2(b − 4), i.e. b + 1 = 2b − 8, giving b = 9. Then Alex = 9 + 5 = 14. Check: 4 years ago Alex was 10, Ben was 5, and 10 = 2 × 5.
